name: session-reset
description: Claude session runs a no-op turn, then the user invokes /clear. The old session row should disappear from the dashboard and a new session_id should appear in ready. Adapter-specific note: claudecode's /clear creates a new transcript file under the same project slug; the driver's reset_session step auto-detects the new UUID and re-points subsequent turns at it. Coverage notes a known partial caveat (#169 stale PID metadata) — if the daemon retains the old session_id past /clear, this recipe surfaces that as a fail on the old_session_ended phase.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: n/a
driver_capability: n/a

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: n/a
driver_capability: n/a

## Scenario

`session-reset` requires the agent's in-process reset command to **end the current session and start a fresh one with a different `session_id`** without relaunching the binary. The user-observable signal is: the old session row disappears and a new row appears with a *different* `session_id` in `ready`. Primitive exercised: `slash_commands` (`capabilities.json` key `slash_commands`, true for aider).

## Reasoning

### aider has reset slash commands, but they clear context — they do not mint a session

The coverage note's stated reason ("aider has no /clear analogue") is factually wrong: aider 0.86.2 ships both `/clear` and `/reset`. But the verdict it lands on (`no`) is still correct, for the reason the `scenarios.json` scope_note already records.

- `Commands.cmd_clear` → `_clear_chat_history()` sets `self.coder.done_messages = []` and `self.coder.cur_messages = []` — in-memory Python lists only.
- `Commands.cmd_reset` drops added files and clears chat history — also purely in-memory.

Neither command touches the on-disk `.aider.chat.history.md`, and neither creates any new identity. They wipe the LLM context window the next turn is sent, nothing more.

### aider has no `session_id` concept at all

A repo-wide grep of the aider source for `session_id` / `session id` returns zero hits. aider has no per-conversation session identity to change — `session_id_assignment: false` in `capabilities.json`. There is nothing for a reset command to re-mint, so the scenario's defining signal (a *different* `session_id`) is structurally impossible. Compared to the codex `/new` supersession case — where a NEW session row supersedes the old on the same PID — aider's `/reset` produces NO session boundary at all: one PID continues, one append-only transcript continues. There is no two-row event to represent.

### Why this is `agent_supports: no` (axis routing under #476)

The blocking axis is the AGENT, not the daemon parser or the driver. aider lacks the feature the scenario exercises — a reset that mints a new session identity — so `agent_supports: no`. With the feature absent there is no observation question (`daemon_capability: n/a`) and no recipe to drive (`driver_capability: n/a`). This is NOT a daemon `incapable` (the daemon isn't being asked to observe a real boundary aider produces but can't represent — aider produces no boundary), and NOT a driver `gap:reset_session` (the driver's missing `reset_session` step is moot when there is no session to reset into a new identity). Per the Step 6 routing table, `no` → `n/a` / `n/a` → **frozen**.

### The transport keys identity on the file path, which is invariant

The adapter is `FilesUnderCWD{Filename: ".aider.chat.history.md"}`. aider opens this single file in append mode (`open("a")` in `io.py:1131`) for the life of the project — one file per project, written to forever. The daemon's session identity for aider is therefore the transcript file path, and `/clear` / `/reset` do not change it. The parser's only file-level marker, the `# aider chat started` header line, is an explicit no-op (`parser.go` returns `nil` for it).

Quitting and relaunching aider does append a fresh `# aider chat started` banner to the *same* file — that is `session-end` + `session-start` on one identity, the "Not to be confused with" case the scenario explicitly excludes, not a session-reset.

## Conclusion

With no session-id primitive and a single append-only transcript per project, aider cannot produce the old-row-disappears / new-`session_id`-appears signal in-process. `agent_supports: no` ⇒ `daemon_capability: n/a` (nothing to observe) and `driver_capability: n/a` (no recipe to drive). Pipeline frozen; no recipe, no recording.
caveats: Coverage note text is inaccurate: aider DOES have /clear and /reset (aider 0.86.2). The verdict (no) is correct — those commands clear in-memory chat context without minting a session id — but the maintainer should fix the note to say 'reset commands clear chat history but mint no session id; aider has no session_id concept'.

sources.note: Source is FilesUnderCWD with Filename .aider.chat.history.md — one transcript per project CWD; identity is the file path.
Parser emits user_message / assistant_message / tool_result / turn_done; '# aider chat started' header is an explicit no-op (line 88). No session_id handling anywhere.
session_id_assignment: false, session_resume: false; slash_commands: true (the Primitive-exercised key — present, but reset commands mint no session id).
cmd_clear -> _clear_chat_history() resets done_messages/cur_messages in memory; cmd_reset drops files + clears history. Neither touches the transcript file or mints an id. aider 0.86.2.
append_chat_history opens chat_history_file in append mode; single per-project file written for the whole run.
default_chat_history_file = <git_root>/.aider.chat.history.md — one file per project, no per-session variant.

recipe.scope_note: Coverage matrix declares aider supports=no for session-reset: aider has /clear (clears chat history) but it does NOT mint a new session id — there is no aider session-id concept. Closing aider creates a fresh `.aider.chat.history.md` on next launch but that's session-end + session-start, not session-reset.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

Claude Code's `/clear` slash command resets the conversation in-place: claude generates a fresh session UUID, materializes a new transcript file under the same `~/.claude/projects/<slug>/` directory, and the old UUID's transcript is closed. Spec assertions:

1. **New session appears in ready with a NEW session_id.** Observable: the daemon's fswatcher detects the new UUID-keyed JSONL via `transcript_new`, the session_detector emits a fresh `session_birth` with `new_session: true`.
2. **Old session disappears.** Observable: PIDManager's same-PID cleanup emits `KindTranscriptRemoved` for the prior UUID (fixed in #169, commit 56c4667 — the fix is what made this scenario fully observable; pre-fix the old session would linger as a zombie until the readyTTL sweep).
3. **Subsequent turns drive the NEW session ready→working→ready normally.** Observable: standard turn arc on the new UUID, asserted via `same_session_as: v2_session_birth` in the phase chain.

The committed recording is 9/9 phases passing. The phase DSL precisely models the pre-session proc-PID → UUID-1 → /clear → UUID-2 lifecycle, including bounding the visible overlap window (the daemon emits the new session's `transcript_new` BEFORE the old session's `transcript_removed` by ~50ms because cleanup triggers at `pid_discovered` time which arrives after the new transcript appears; the `old_session_ended` phase's `max_delay_ms: 1000` relative to `v2_session_birth` bounds this overlap).

Eliminating the 50ms overlap entirely would break the legitimate `multiple-sessions-same-cwd` case (different PIDs in same projectDir), so the spec accepts the small window. This trade-off is documented in expected.jsonl's notes field.

## What irrlicht does NOT see (and why that's narrow)

Claude's `/new` slash command (the second variant the prose spec mentions) is functionally identical to `/clear` at the transport level — same UUID rotation, same transcript_removed for the old, same new_session for the new. The recipe covers `/clear` only; `/new` would re-record identically.
caveats: Assessment backfilled in 2026-05-18 to fill the Stage-1 artifact gap. The recording + spec predate the assessment.json convention (introduced in commit de926ab); the verdict here is consistent with the existing 9/9 pass-rate.
Issue #169 (PIDManager cleanup emitting transcript_removed) was the load-bearing fix that made this scenario observable; pre-0.4.x daemons would not have passed.
The /clear → /new alternative form is functionally identical at the transport level but is not separately recorded.

sources.note: Same-PID cleanup that emits KindTranscriptRemoved for the rotated-out UUID — the #169 fix.
Session detector emits session_birth with new_session: true on a fresh UUID's first user-line.
Feature: Session reset (`/clear`, `/new`) L81-88 — three Expected: bullets all observable.
Pre-fix the old session would linger as a zombie; the fix added the explicit transcript_removed emit.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated
irrlichd recording (--attach against --record daemon or isolated mode)
setup: Driver generates a fresh UUID for the first session; the reset_session step discovers the second UUID by scanning the project slug dir after /clear lands.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
Reply with exactly the word: fresh

verify: events.jsonl shows the FIRST UUID session entering ready, then a working/ready cycle for turn 1
after /clear, events.jsonl emits transcript_removed (and/or process_exited) for the first UUID — the 'old session disappears' assertion from the spec
events.jsonl contains a DIFFERENT UUID session appearing in ready after /clear (new session_id, fresh row)
the second turn's working/ready cycle is on the NEW UUID, not the old one
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains exactly 2 user lines and 2 assistant lines (the two transcripts are concatenated by the multi-session curate path)

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## agent_supports: yes

Codex's reset command is `/new`: it starts a fresh conversation that SUPERSEDES the current one, without relaunching the binary. The fresh session's rollout (`~/.codex/sessions/.../rollout-<ts>-<uuid2>.jsonl`, a distinct `session_meta.id`) materializes lazily on the first post-reset prompt and contains none of the pre-reset history. (`/clear` produces the same observable supersession and is what the committed recording was driven with; both rotate to a brand-new rollout UUID.)

## daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

Reset for codex is supersession: a new session replaces the old one, and that IS the expected, correct behavior. The daemon (codex Source = FilesUnderRoot watching ~/.codex/sessions — agent.go:32) detects the new rollout the instant it appears and emits transcript_new + a state_transition to ready + pid_discovered under a session_id never seen before. That fresh session_id appearing in ready is the complete, user-observable reset signal — exactly the scenario's assertion 'a new session row appears with a different session_id in ready'.

The committed recording captures the full arc: proc-<PID> pre-session → first rollout (UUID-1) runs a turn → reset → second rollout (UUID-2, fresh session_id) runs a turn. expected.jsonl encodes it as 8 phases and validates 8/8.

## Why there is no old_session_ended phase (and why that is correct)

codex actually reaches the SAME removal path as claudecode. Both rollouts share one PID (28434 in the recording), so when the post-reset rollout (UUID-2) is PID-discovered the daemon's same-PID cleanup (pid_manager.go:373-399, issue #169 / commit 56c4667 — a non-subagent PID can only belong to one session at a time) immediately deletes UUID-1 and records transcript_removed: seq 1602 at 00:27:12.103, ~7ms after UUID-2's pid_discovered (seq 1601) and ~280ms after v2_session_birth — NOT the readyTTL idle sweep (that path is gated by IsStale(readyTTL) at pid_manager.go:634 and cannot fire this fast).

So why is there no old_session_ended phase? NOT because codex fails to produce a removal — it produces one, via the same #169 path as claudecode. The removal is simply not anchored to the reset keystroke: codex reset is LAZY, so the new rollout, its PID bind, and the resulting #169 cleanup all land together on the first post-reset prompt (the old rollout FILE meanwhile lingers frozen on disk — only the daemon's session row is removed). expected.jsonl asserts the canonical reset proxy — a fresh session_id appearing in ready (v2_session_birth) — across its 8 phases; the old-session removal is present in events.jsonl (seq 1602) but left unasserted as a scoping choice, not because it is absent. The daemon fully observes the reset — daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready.
caveats: Codex's reset command is /new; the committed recording was driven via the reset_session step (which sends /clear) — both produce the same observable: the old rollout freezes and a fresh rollout with a new session_id appears. Switching the driver's reset_session to /new for label fidelity is an optional follow-up that would need a re-record.

The old session IS removed — via the same #169 same-PID cleanup as claudecode (both rollouts share one PID; transcript_removed for UUID-1 lands ~7ms after the new rollout's pid_discovered, seq 1602). It is just not anchored to the reset keystroke (codex reset is lazy, so removal fires on the first post-reset prompt). No old_session_ended phase is asserted — a fixture scoping choice, since v2_session_birth is the canonical reset proxy — not because codex emits no removal.

sources.note: Codex CLI slash commands — /new starts a fresh chat (session reset).

Agent() constructor sets Source = FilesUnderRoot watching ~/.codex/sessions (agent.go:32) — any new rollout under the root is detected, so a reset-spawned new session is daemon-observable as a fresh session_id.
DiscoverPID binds the codex PID via transcript-writer; both pre- and post-reset sessions share one PID — the precondition for the #169 same-PID cleanup that removes the old session.

Lines 373-399: same-PID cleanup (issue #169) — when a new session claims a PID already held by an old session, the old one is deleted and transcript_removed is recorded immediately. This retires codex's UUID-1 ~7ms after the new rollout's pid_discovered (NOT the readyTTL sweep at line 634, gated by IsStale(readyTTL)).
Committed recording: two distinct rollout session_ids (UUID-1, UUID-2) + proc-PID pre-session; expected.jsonl validates 8/8.
Reset command is /new; new-replaces-old is expected behavior — absent reset-time removal is not a gap.

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.codex/config.toml or OPENAI_API_KEY) — verified codex 0.130.0
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

PARTIAL observability (per assessment.json): codex /clear does NOT emit a transcript_removed for the old session — the old rollout lingers frozen until process_exited. The 'old session disappears' spec bullet has no codex-side removal event; only the 'new session appears' signal is asserted.

KNOWN CAPTURE GAP: drive-codex-interactive.sh caches the first rollout and writes a single session.uuid, so curate-lifecycle-fixture.sh filters the fixture to UUID-1 only. The v2_* (post-/clear) phases assert what the daemon observes LIVE; they may be absent from the single-session curated fixture, surfacing as validation drift until the codex driver tracks /clear-spawned sessions (a developer task).
setup: Driver launches codex in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ (isolates the trust dialog).

Codex assigns its own session UUID per rollout; there is no --session-id flag. The first prompt materializes rollout-<ts>-<uuid1>.jsonl; the first prompt AFTER /clear materializes a second rollout-<ts>-<uuid2>.jsonl with a fresh session_id and no UUID-1 history.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
Reply with exactly the word: fresh

verify: events.jsonl shows a proc-<PID> pre-session entering ready, handing off to the FIRST rollout-keyed session in ready
events.jsonl shows the FIRST rollout session running a working→ready turn (turn 1, 'ok')

after /clear, the first post-clear prompt spawns a DIFFERENT rollout session_id in ready (new session row, no UUID-1 history) — the 'old session disappears / new session appears' reset, observed live on the dashboard
the second turn's working→ready cycle is on the NEW rollout session, not the old one
codex does NOT emit transcript_removed for the old session at /clear time (the old rollout lingers frozen) — the old row stays ready until process_exited; do not assert a removal event
final live state is ready

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready
route: record (already RECORDED 2026-05-26; this reassessment ratifies it)

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready
route: record (already RECORDED 2026-05-26; this reassessment ratifies it)

## Scenario

session-reset: a running session completes a turn, then the user issues an in-process reset (`/new`); the old session row disappears and a fresh `session_id` is born in `ready`, all WITHOUT relaunching the binary. Distinct from session-end + session-start (two separate processes/invocations) and from checkpoint-rewind (which keeps the same `session_id`). Primitive exercised: `slash_commands`.

## Agent supports it (agent_supports: yes)

opencode's interactive TUI ships a `/new` reset command. `strings $(which opencode)` on 1.14.50 surfaces the literal `/new` and `/clear` slash tokens, plus the TUI's new-session machinery (`onNewSession`, `starting new session`, `new sessions unavailable`). In a live TUI the user resets the conversation to a fresh `ses_…` mid-session — exactly this scenario.

## Daemon observes it (daemon_capability: full)

opencode is the lone `ProcessOwnedStore` adapter (`core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/agent.go` — `Source: agent.ProcessOwnedStore{Reader: metricsReader{}}`): the daemon reads session state from the SQLite store, so a `/new`-spawned `ses_…` is observed in `ready` like any other session. The committed recording (`events.jsonl`) proves the full reset arc live: a transient `proc-<PID>` pre-session is born ready at launch and hands off to the first `ses_…` (turn 1 working→ready, 'ok'), then `/new` mints a SECOND `ses_…` (`transcript_new` for `ses_19ae7f93…`), the daemon RETIRES the old row (`transcript_removed` for `ses_19ae8f9f…` — the 'old session disappears' signal, which opencode emits and codex's `/clear` does NOT), turn 2 runs working→ready on the new session ('fresh'), then `process_exited`. expected-validate passes 7/7 phases (`v1_session_handoff`, `v1_turn_start/end`, `v2_session_birth`, `v2_turn_start/end`).

## Driver: ready (the `gap:slash-command` is stale)

This is the load-bearing change from the prior coverage verdict. The matrix still records `driver_capability: gap:slash-command` (tracked #492), but that gap is now CLOSED. `drive-opencode-interactive.sh` carries a `run_live()` path: the top-level router (`if any(.[]; .type == "reset_session" or .type == "slash")`) sends any reset_session/slash recipe to a live opencode TUI under tmux, which delivers `/new` as a real in-REPL command via `tmux send-keys` (`oc_reset`), detects turn completion by polling the SQLite store for new `step-finish` parts (`oc_wait_turn` / `oc_turn_total`), and enumerates both top-level `ses_…` rows for the run cwd to emit the multi-session contract (`session.uuids` / `transcript.paths`) so curate keeps the pre- AND post-reset sessions. The recipe in `scenarios.json` uses a `reset_session` step, which the driver implements end-to-end.

The behavior is NOT recordable via the simpler headless paths — confirmed, not assumed: headless `opencode run "/new"` stores `/new` as ordinary user prompt text and mints only ONE `ses_…` (no reset), and a 'fresh session' would be session-start, not an in-process reset. So an in-REPL slash primitive IS required — and the driver now HAS it. The driver gap was the only thing missing; it is no longer missing.

## Confidence

0.9: empirically recorded and validated (7/7 expected phases; replay golden present), the driver code implementing the reset_session primitive is in the tree and inspected line-by-line, and the agent's `/new` is confirmed in the 1.14.50 binary strings. The full supersession arc — including the old-session `transcript_removed` — is in events.jsonl.
caveats: The reset minting is lazy: opencode creates the new `ses_…` row on the first prompt AFTER `/new`, not on `/new` itself (the recipe's post-reset send materializes it). This mirrors the initial session and codex's post-/clear rollout, and is why the `v2_session_birth` phase allows up to 30s.

Live-TUI cell: the driver routes session-reset (and any reset_session/slash recipe) through a tmux-driven opencode TUI because headless `opencode run` cannot deliver in-REPL slash commands. tmux must be on PATH at record time.

Stronger than codex/pi supersession: opencode DOES emit `transcript_removed` for the retired old session (same-PID cleanup), so the 'old session disappears' bullet is directly observable here, not just inferred from the new session appearing.

sources.note: run_live() path (lines 99-294) + the router at line 355 (any reset_session/slash step → live TUI): tmux send-keys for prompts, /new via oc_reset, step-finish polling for wait_turn, multi-session session.uuids/transcript.paths contract — the reset_session primitive is implemented, closing the prior gap:slash-command

recorded live arc: proc-23009 pre-session → ses_19ae8f9f handoff (turn 1) → transcript_new ses_19ae7f93 (/new reset) → transcript_removed ses_19ae8f9f (old session retired) → turn 2 on ses_19ae7f93 → process_exited
7 phases (v1 handoff/turn, v2 session birth/turn) — manifest records 7/7 phases passed, so daemon_capability:full is measured, not inferred
Source: agent.ProcessOwnedStore{Reader: metricsReader{}} — opencode is the lone SQLite-store adapter; daemon reads session state from the store, so a /new-spawned ses_… appears in ready

sources.ref: opencode run "/new" + opencode TUI under tmux (2026-05-26)
sources.note: headless `opencode run "/new"` stored /new as user text and minted ONE ses_… (no reset) → an in-REPL slash primitive is genuinely required; the TUI /new mints a second ses_… under the same process on the next prompt (RECORDABLE)
strings scan surfaces /new and /clear slash tokens plus onNewSession / 'starting new session' TUI machinery → agent_supports:yes
coverage cell still says driver_capability:gap:slash-command (notes ref #492); it should update to driver_capability:ready — the driver's run_live path closed that gap and the cell is recorded

preconditions: opencode on PATH and authenticated (~/.local/share/opencode/auth.json) — verified opencode 1.14.50. The session-reset arc is model-agnostic; the dev env's default is a local LM Studio model, which is fine.
irrlichd is recording — run-cell.sh isolated mode, or --attach against a daemon started with --record.

LIVE-TUI cell: drive-opencode-interactive.sh routes any recipe carrying a reset_session step to its tmux TUI path, because headless `opencode run` stores a slash command as literal prompt text (PROBED 2026-05-26: `opencode run "/new"` minted ONE ses_… and stored `/new` as a user message). tmux must be on PATH.
setup: Driver launches the opencode TUI in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/; opencode keys sessions on the cwd in its SQLite store, isolating this run's rows even alongside other opencode use.

opencode mints its own ses_… per session. The first prompt materializes ses_1; an in-TUI `/new` clears the conversation and the first prompt AFTER it materializes a fresh ses_2 under the SAME process. The driver enumerates both top-level ses_… rows for the cwd and emits the multi-session contract (session.uuids / transcript.paths), so curate includes the pre- AND post-reset sessions.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
Reply with exactly the word: fresh

verify: events.jsonl shows the FIRST ses_… session entering ready, then a working→ready turn (turn 1, 'ok')

after /new, the first post-reset prompt mints a DIFFERENT ses_… in ready (new session row, no turn-1 history) — the 'old session disappears / new session appears' reset, observed live; opencode retires the old row via the same-PID cleanup like the codex/pi supersession shape
the second working→ready cycle is on the NEW ses_… session, not the old one
final live state is ready

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

This reassessment FLIPPED the prior `applicable: false` cell to yes/yes. The earlier scope_note ("Pi's TUI does not document a /clear or /new command that resets the in-process session") was wrong: pi 0.70.0 DOES ship `/new`.

## agent_supports: yes

Pi's reset command is `/new` (verified live against pi 0.70.0). In the interactive REPL, `/new` is handled at interactive-mode.js:2037 → `handleClearCommand()` (interactive-mode.js:4360) → `runtimeHost.newSession()` (agent-session-runtime.js:142) → `sessionManager.newSession()` (session-manager.js:484). `newSession()` mints a fresh `createSessionId()` UUID and a new `<ts>_<uuid2>.jsonl` session-file path under the same project dir, clears the in-memory entries (parentSession defaults to null — no UUID-1 history), and keeps the SAME process alive (no relaunch). The file flushes lazily on the first post-reset message — the exact codex `/new` supersession shape: the NEW session supersedes the OLD without restarting the binary.

Empirically confirmed: a standalone driver run (send 'ok' → /new → send 'fresh') produced TWO distinct rollout files under one PID — UUID-1 (`...744Z_019e5cbd...`, the 'ok' turn, frozen after /new) and UUID-2 (`...431Z_019e5cbe...`, header parentSession:null, the 'fresh' turn, no UUID-1 history).

## daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

Reset for pi is supersession, and new-replaces-old IS the expected, correct behavior. The daemon (pi Source = FilesUnderRoot watching ~/.pi/agent/sessions — agent.go) detects UUID-2 the instant its file appears and emits transcript_new + a state_transition to ready + pid_discovered under a session_id never seen before. That fresh session_id appearing in ready is the complete user-observable reset signal — exactly the scenario's 'a new session row appears with a different session_id in ready'. Both rollouts share one PID, so when UUID-2 is PID-discovered the same-PID cleanup (#169 — the SAME path as claudecode/codex) removes UUID-1's daemon row ~ms after the new pid_discovered (NOT a readyTTL idle sweep). The old-session removal is thus produced (just not anchored to the reset keystroke — reset is lazy, so it lands on the first post-reset prompt; the old FILE lingers frozen on disk).

## Why this cell is committed as by_adapter.pi.applicable:false despite irrlicht_observes:yes

The DAEMON observes the reset fully and LIVE. The blocker is purely the FIXTURE-CAPTURE driver: `drive-pi-interactive.sh` lacks the codex-style multi-session machinery (codex's `reset_session` step re-arms resolve_transcript onto the new rollout slot and writes a `session.uuids` PLURAL list so curate-lifecycle-fixture.sh keeps BOTH UUIDs). The pi driver caches `$TRANSCRIPT` once and writes only the singular `session.uuid` (UUID-1). Consequences proven by the dry-run: (1) the second `wait_turn` reads the stale UUID-1 transcript and TIMES OUT (turn count stuck at 1); (2) curate filters the recording to `session.uuid` (UUID-1) + proc-PID + children, so UUID-2's events are dropped — a curated fixture can only ever contain UUID-1 and the v2_* phases would be absent. Extending the pi interactive driver with reset_session/session.uuids capture is a developer task (out of scope for the implement stage, which only authors recipes). Recording is DEFERRED until that lands; expected.jsonl documents the full 8-phase target arc for when it does.
caveats: Pi's reset command is `/new` (interactive-mode.js:2037). It maps to handleClearCommand → newSession, which mints a fresh UUID file under the same PID — the codex supersession pattern. The old rollout file lingers frozen; the daemon removes the old session row via the #169 same-PID cleanup once UUID-2's PID is discovered.

irrlicht_observes is YES at the daemon level. applicable:false is a CAPTURE-tooling limitation, not an observability gap: drive-pi-interactive.sh has no codex-style reset_session / session.uuids multi-session tracking, so (a) the post-/new wait_turn times out on the stale transcript and (b) curate can only retain UUID-1, dropping UUID-2 from the fixture. The behavior is real and recordable once the driver gains multi-session capture.

sources.note: Line 2037: `/new` → editor.setText('') + handleClearCommand(). Line 4360: handleClearCommand → runtimeHost.newSession(). Confirms pi 0.70.0 ships a /new reset that keeps the process alive.

Line 484 newSession(): mints createSessionId() + a new <ts>_<uuid>.jsonl path, clears fileEntries (parentSession null), flushed=false (lazy materialization) — fresh UUID file, same process, no prior history.
Pi Source = FilesUnderRoot watching ~/.pi/agent/sessions — any new rollout file under the root is daemon-detected, so a /new-spawned UUID-2 surfaces as a fresh session_id in ready.

Same-PID cleanup (issue #169): when UUID-2 claims the PID already held by UUID-1, the old session row is removed and transcript_removed recorded immediately — the SAME path that retires codex's/claudecode's superseded session, not the readyTTL idle sweep.

Caches $TRANSCRIPT once (resolve_transcript returns early when set) and writes only singular session.uuid — no reset_session step, no session.uuids list. This is the capture gap that forces applicable:false: the post-/new wait_turn times out and curate keeps only UUID-1.
Reference: codex driver's reset_session step + SES_TRANSCRIPT slots + session.uuids plural — the multi-session capture the pi driver would need to record this cell.

session-reset flipped to yes/yes (0.72) on the codex /new supersession pattern; deferred to applicable:false because the pi interactive driver lacks codex-style multi-session capture (a developer task), not because the daemon fails to observe the reset.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (~/.pi/ credentials) — verified pi 0.70.0, which ships the in-REPL `/new` reset (interactive-mode.js:2037 → handleClearCommand → runtimeHost.newSession → session-manager.js:484 newSession()).
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record.

FULL observability (per assessment.json yes/yes 0.72): pi /new is the codex supersession shape — newSession() mints a fresh createSessionId() UUID and a new <ts>_<uuid2>.jsonl under the same PID (parentSession:null, no UUID-1 history, file flushed lazily on the first post-reset message). The daemon (pi Source = FilesUnderRoot ~/.pi/agent/sessions) detects UUID-2 the instant its file appears: a fresh session_id in ready is the user-observable reset signal. The old UUID-1 row is removed via the #169 same-PID cleanup once UUID-2's PID is discovered (~ms after the new pid_discovered, NOT a readyTTL sweep); the old UUID-1 FILE lingers frozen on disk. No transcript_removed fires at the /new keystroke itself (reset is lazy — UUID-2 materializes on the first post-reset prompt).
setup: Driver launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/.

Pi mints its own session UUID per rollout. The first prompt materializes <ts>_<uuid1>.jsonl; the reset_session step sends pi's in-REPL `/new` (allocating a new driver slot reusing the same tmux/process), and the first prompt AFTER /new materializes a second <ts>_<uuid2>.jsonl with a fresh session_id and no UUID-1 history. The driver re-arms resolve_transcript onto UUID-2 and records BOTH UUIDs into session.uuids / transcript.paths so curate keeps both rollouts.

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
Reply with exactly the word: fresh

verify: events.jsonl shows a proc-<PID> pre-session entering ready, handing off to the FIRST rollout-keyed session (UUID-1) in ready
events.jsonl shows the FIRST rollout session running a working→ready turn (turn 1, 'ok')

after /new, the first post-reset prompt spawns a DIFFERENT rollout session_id (UUID-2, parentSession:null, no UUID-1 history) in ready — the 'new session appears with a different session_id' reset, observed live on the dashboard
the second turn's working→ready cycle is on the NEW rollout session (UUID-2), not on stale UUID-1

the old UUID-1 daemon row is removed via the #169 same-PID cleanup once UUID-2's PID is discovered (the old UUID-1 file lingers frozen on disk); no transcript_removed fires at the /new keystroke itself
no waiting state appears at any point; final live state is ready
